items.prompt: Candidates are sorted, and a combination loop is choosing values at one recursion depth. Which rule skips value-equivalent sibling branches?

options.text: Skip candidates[i] when i > start and candidates[i] equals candidates[i - 1].
Skip every value equal to any value already in the current path.
Skip the first occurrence and keep only later equal values.
Skip equal neighbors whenever i > 0, regardless of the current start.

feedback.reason: Sorted equal values should produce one sibling branch per value at the same depth.
feedback.details: The i > start condition scopes duplicate skipping to sibling choices while still allowing equal values to appear at different depths when legal.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_all_equal_globally: Equal values may be legal at different depths when multiplicity permits.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_first_duplicate: The first occurrence should represent the value choice at this depth.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_if_i_gt_zero: This can incorrectly remove a legal repeated value at a deeper level.

items.prompt: In unique-permutation generation with sorted values and a used array, when should a candidate equal to the previous value be skipped?

options.text: When the previous equal index is not used in the current path.
When the previous equal index is already used in the current path.
Whenever an equal value exists anywhere in the input.
Equal candidates never need special handling.

feedback.reason: If the previous equal occurrence is unused, choosing the later one would start a sibling branch equivalent to choosing the earlier one.
feedback.details: When the previous equal occurrence is already in the current path, the later occurrence can represent the next distinct input position.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.previous_equal_used: That usage distinguishes an ordering within the current branch and should remain legal.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.always_equal: This would prevent using multiple equal occurrences when the input multiplicity allows them.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.never_equal: They can generate value-identical sibling permutations.

items.prompt: Why is skipping equal values at every depth unsafe for combinations that may use two copies of the same value?

options.text: It can remove a legal branch that selects separate equal occurrences at different depths.
Equal values are never allowed in a combination.
Sorted input cannot be processed recursively.
Equal values always have the same index.

feedback.reason: Duplicate control should eliminate equivalent choice orderings without erasing permitted multiplicity.
feedback.details: Same-depth skipping preserves one representative sibling, while deeper selection can still use another occurrence.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.values_cannot_repeat: Multiplicity depends on the input and result contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_breaks_recursion: Sorting is compatible with recursive search.

items.prompt: An input contains [1a, 1b, 2], where 1a and 1b have equal values but distinct indices. What distinction must the solver preserve before applying value-level deduplication?

options.text: The two occurrences are separate candidate positions even though their values are equal.
They are automatically the same candidate object.
Only one occurrence exists logically.
Sorting makes their indices identical.

feedback.reason: Candidate identity and result equivalence are different layers.
feedback.details: The solver may later suppress value-equivalent outputs, but it must understand the available multiplicity represented by distinct indices.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_object: Equal values do not imply identical positions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.one_count_only: The input explicitly contains two occurrences.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_identity: Sorting reorders positions but does not merge occurrences.

items.prompt: Why is generating every index permutation and deduplicating completed value sequences with a set usually inferior to preventing duplicate branches?

options.text: It explores many equivalent branches and pays to hash or compare completed outputs.
A set necessarily changes the values inside each permutation.
Result deduplication makes recursion impossible.
Proactive control always requires illegal sorting.

feedback.reason: Proactive duplicate control reduces the search tree before equivalent work is performed.
feedback.details: A result set can be a correctness fallback, but it does not recover the time and memory spent generating duplicates.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.set_changes_values: A set can store sequences without modifying their elements.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.recursion_impossible: The method is possible but inefficient.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_not_allowed: Sorting is legal when input order is not part of the output contract.

items.prompt: Why is sorting often useful before duplicate-controlled combination search?

options.text: It places equal values next to each other so same-depth duplicate checks are local.
It automatically deletes duplicate occurrences.
It makes the full backtracking search linear.
It makes branch-state restoration unnecessary.

feedback.reason: Adjacent equal values allow a deterministic previous-value or i > start skip rule.
feedback.details: Sorting must still be compatible with the result contract; for combinations and permutations of values, original input order is usually not part of the output.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.eliminates_undo: Sorting does not change mutable path semantics.

items.prompt: In unique permutations of [1, 1, 2], why may the second 1 be chosen after the first 1 is already used in the current path?

options.text: It represents the second distinct input occurrence at a deeper position.
Duplicate control is disabled after the first choice.
The two values become unequal once one is used.
Used positions are always available again.

feedback.reason: Value-equivalent sibling starts are suppressed, but legal multiplicity inside one permutation remains.
feedback.details: The previous-equal-used condition distinguishes a deeper use of another occurrence from an equivalent sibling branch.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_rule_disabled: Duplicate control remains active but is depth-sensitive.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.used_means_available: Used positions cannot be selected twice.

items.prompt: A combinations solver skips a candidate whenever its value already appears anywhere in the current path. What valid result can this wrongly remove?

options.text: A combination containing two separate allowed occurrences of the same value.
Only combinations with all distinct values.
The empty combination.
Every sorted combination.

feedback.reason: Path-level value uniqueness is stronger than same-depth output deduplication.
feedback.details: The correct rule depends on whether the result contract allows repeated values from distinct input occurrences.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct_only: Those do not contain a repeated path value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.empty_combination: No candidate skip is evaluated for an already complete empty path.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly separates duplicate control from visited-state control?

options.text: Duplicate control suppresses equivalent outputs; visited state prevents illegal reuse within one path.
They are always the same condition.
Visited state exists only to deduplicate completed results.
Duplicate control exists only for graph cycles.

feedback.reason: One mechanism preserves result uniqueness, while the other preserves path legality.
feedback.details: They may interact in a duplicate-permutation solver, but they must not be conflated.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.visited_only_outputs: Visited state usually enforces branch legality.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_only_cycles: It commonly appears in combinations and permutations.

items.prompt: Which techniques can prevent duplicate combination results? Select all that apply.

options.text: Sort candidates and skip equal sibling choices at the same recursion depth.
Advance the start index so the same index set is not generated in different orders.
Allow equal values at different depths only when distinct occurrences and the contract permit them.
Reject every candidate whose value appears anywhere in the path.

feedback.reason: Unique combination generation requires both order canonicalization and depth-scoped value handling.
feedback.details: A global no-equal-values rule is not a general substitute.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.skip_equal_any_depth: This can remove valid repeated-value combinations.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.sort_same_depth_skip: This chooses one representative branch per value at that depth.

items.prompt: Which conditions belong to the standard sorted unique-permutation skip rule? Select all that apply.

options.text: The candidate has a previous input position.
The candidate value equals the previous sorted value.
The previous equal position is not used in the current path.
The current index itself is unused.

feedback.reason: Unique-permutation legality combines ordinary unused-index state with a duplicate sibling condition.
feedback.details: Omitting any part can either admit duplicates or remove legal multiplicity.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.i_greater_zero: A previous equal value must exist before it can define an equivalent sibling.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.equal_previous: Only equal values create value-equivalent starts.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.previous_not_used: That means the earlier equal value was available to start the equivalent sibling branch.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.current_unused: Every permutation candidate must be unused.

items.prompt: Which test inputs are useful for duplicate-control review? Select all that apply.

options.text: All values are distinct.
All values are equal.
Some values repeat while others are unique.
Only a single trivial input and no other cases.

feedback.reason: Review should cover no duplicates, maximal duplicates, and mixed multiplicity.
feedback.details: These shapes expose both under-skipping and over-skipping.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_one_value: One case cannot expose depth-scoping defects.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_distinct: It verifies that duplicate logic does not remove ordinary branches.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_equal: It stresses maximal branch equivalence and multiplicity.

items.prompt: Which statements distinguish proactive duplicate prevention from result-set deduplication? Select all that apply.

options.text: Proactive prevention can avoid exploring equivalent branches.
Result-set deduplication removes repeated outputs only after they are generated.
A result set may require hashing and storing completed outputs.
Both methods necessarily visit the same number of search states.

feedback.reason: The approaches may produce the same final value set but have different search and memory costs.
feedback.details: Proactive rules are preferable when they can be proven complete.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.same_performance: Proactive control can visit far fewer states.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.prevention_smaller_tree: The search tree itself becomes smaller.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.result_set_after_work: Equivalent search work has already occurred.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.set_memory: Deduplication has additional memory and comparison cost.

items.prompt: Arrange the duplicate-controlled combination candidate flow for sorted input.

elements.text: Iterate candidate indices from the current start boundary.
If i > start and candidates[i] equals candidates[i - 1], skip this sibling.
Choose the candidate and recurse with start = i + 1.
Undo the choice and continue to the next index.

feedback.reason: The duplicate check is evaluated in the parent loop before creating an equivalent sibling branch.
feedback.details: Passing i + 1 preserves combination ordering, while undo restores the path.

items.prompt: A multiset contains n values with multiplicities c1, c2, ..., cm. Select the number of distinct full permutations and maximum recursion depth.
constraints: All occurrences are used exactly once.
Outputs differing only by swapping equal values are considered identical.

feedback.reason: Equal-value swaps do not create new value sequences, reducing n! indexed permutations by multiplicity factorials.
feedback.details: A complete output still has n positions, so recursion depth remains linear.

items.prompt: An implementation generates all n! indexed permutations, copies each length-n path, and deduplicates them afterward, even though only u value-distinct permutations exist. Select an upper bound on generation-and-copy work and final output space.
constraints: Every indexed permutation is generated before deduplication.
Each generated path copy has length n.

feedback.reason: Post-hoc deduplication does not avoid generating and copying duplicate indexed paths.
feedback.details: The final retained outputs scale with u, but the wasted generation work still scales with n! paths.
